taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to coastal areas of North and South Carolina in the United States, found within a 75-mile radius of Wilmington, North Carolina.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in wet, acidic, nitrogen-poor soils of longleaf pine savannas and pocosins. Found in sandy or peaty soils with seasonal water table fluctuations, in full sun among grasses and low shrubs. Occurs at elevations near sea level up to about 150 feet.

care.light: Full Sun or at least 20W per sq ft LED light. Venus flytraps love bright light and do best outdoors in full sun
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in 0.5”-1” of water using tray method. Flytraps can tolerate moist to soggy conditions, but do not allow the soil to dry out
care.soil: 1 part peat: 1 part sand, or 1 part peat: 1 part perlite. Pure long-fibered sphagnum moss is also good. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts
care.dormancy: Winter dormancy is necessary for long term health. Plants naturally go dormant in fall if grown outdoors. Throughout winter they will slow growth and shrink in size until spring. If growing indoors, gradually adjust photoperiod to around 9 hours in winter to induce dormancy. Reduce photoperiod and give cooler temperatures.
care.temp: Adaptable to any mild climate. Temperatures between 32-100F are best. Moderate humidity is fine. Avoid growing in enclosed areas without airflow. Winter dormancy is necessary for long term health
care.feeding: Feed indoor traps with fresh/hydrated bugs or insect slurry every month or so. Traps will occasionally reopen from inanimate, dry food — to help the trap you can very gently squeeze it a little to help stimulate digestion. Wait until leaves are fully developed for at least few days before feeding. Feed 25-50% of mature traps at a time to stimulate fast growth. Outdoor plants generally catch enough flies on their own.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to the extreme southwestern corner of Western Australia, with records from Scott River and Windy Harbour.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in black peaty sand on the margins of swamps.

care.temp: Mediterranean (SW Australia). 40-100F, low to moderate humidity.
care.light: Full Sun or 20W per sq ft LED light. Pygmy sundews love bright light.
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in 0.5”-1” of water using tray method.
care.soil: 40% Peat: 60% Perlite/Sand is a good mix. Pygmy Sundews like well draining soils. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts.
care.feeding: Feed leaves with Insect Powder every 2-4 weeks. Add an addon gemmae kit (Pygmy Sundew Soil + Pot + Tray) to combine with these gemmae for everything you need to get started. Just add water and light!

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to Western Australia, with records from the Porongurup Range north of Albany and Mount Lindesay northwest of Denmark.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in loam-laterite soils in open areas on the higher scree slopes of low jarrah forest.

care.temp: Mediterranean (SW Australia). 40-100F, low to moderate humidity.
care.light: Full Sun or 20W per sq ft LED light. Pygmy sundews love bright light.

Use humidity method (baggie over pot with progressively cut corners) for first few weeks, then acclimate to lower humidity. Will reach flowering size in several months. Cold winter shipping (<40F) includes heat packs automatically if necessary.

care.dormancy: Adjust photoperiod to 9-10 hours in winter, gradually cycling to 14-15 hours in summer. Produces gemmae each fall in response to proper lighting cues.

taxa.nativeRange: Widespread in southeastern Australia and near Albany and Karridale in Western Australia. Also found in New Zealand (North Island: far north and central volcanic plateau; South Island: northwestern and southern tips). The only pygmy Drosera found outside of Western Australia.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in a wide range of nutrient-poor soils and conditions. On the margins of lakes and swamps it can be submerged for weeks at a time. In alpine areas it can be covered by snow for months. In heath near Sydney it occurs on laterite soils that dry out seasonally. In New Zealand, found in sub-alpine ash deposits on the volcanic plateau and in mossy loam in the South Island.

taxa.nativeRange: Found in the Northern Territory and Queensland, Australia, across tropical northern regions.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in seasonally wet sandy soils in eucalypt woodlands and tropical savannas. Occurs in areas with distinct wet and dry seasons, often in shallow depressions that fill during monsoon rains. Found from near sea level to low elevations.

care.temp: Temperatures of 60-95F at all humidity levels.
care.light: Part Sun or at least 10W-15W per sq ft LED light. This species can tolerate brighter light and seems to be fine.
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Keep the soil constantly wet and do not allow it to dry out. This species does best for me with good drainage and shallow trays.
care.soil: 50% Peat Moss: 50% Sand/Perlite. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts.
care.feeding: Feed with MaxSea or Dynagro fertilizer diluted to 1/4 tsp per gallon (or roughly 200-400ppm) every month. Lightly spray the leaves but do not overfeed, especially in undrained containers.

care.temp: Temperatures from 40-100F, all humidity levels. These will naturally slow growth in winter, and can go fully dormant if cold enough. Frost may cause them to die back to the roots

taxa.nativeRange: Found in the Northern Territory and northern Western Australia, with this form from the Kingston Rest area along the Victoria Highway in the Northern Territory.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in seasonally wet sandy soils in tropical savanna woodlands. Occurs in shallow depressions and drainage lines that flood during the monsoon and dry completely during the dry season. Found at low elevations in eucalypt and paperbark woodlands.

care.temp: Temperatures from 50-85F - they prefer some humidity but can adapt to lower humidity.
care.light: Part Sun or 15W per sq ft LED light. These do well under LED lights and will attain great color.
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in ~0.5” of water using tray method. They can also be top watered regularly in humid environments, but the tray method is simpler.
care.soil: Using standard garden store materials, a mix of roughly 20% Peat Moss: 50% Perlite: 30% Vermiculite will work. I like using a mix of Perlite/Volcanic Clay Granules/Vermiculite but lots of granular, well draining mixes will work. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts. Because the roots are shallow, they do not require deep pots but pot height will influence moisture wicking to the surface.
care.feeding: Dust the leaves with Insect Powder every 2-4 weeks to stimulate explosive growth.  Foliar feeding is also good at a frequency of once or twice per month with 1/4 tsp per gallon MaxSea or roughly 500ppm Dynagro Gro/Foliage Pro or Schultz Cactus Fertilizer.

care.light: Part Sun or 15W per sq ft LED light. These do well under LED lights and will attain great color.
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in ~0.5” of water using tray method. They can also be top watered regularly in humid environments, but the tray method is simpler.
care.soil: Using standard garden store materials, a mix of roughly 20% Peat Moss: 50% Perlite: 30% Vermiculite will work. I like using a mix of Perlite/Volcanic Clay Granules/Vermiculite but lots of granular, well draining mixes will work. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts. Because the roots are shallow, they do not require deep pots but pot height will influence moisture wicking to the surface.
care.feeding: Dust the leaves with Insect Powder every 2-4 weeks to stimulate explosive growth.  Foliar feeding is also good at a frequency of once or twice per month with 1/4 tsp per gallon MaxSea or roughly 500ppm Dynagro Gro/Foliage Pro or Schultz Cactus Fertilizer.

care.temp: Mediterranean (SW Australia). 40-100F, low to moderate humidity.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to coastal North and South Carolina, United States. Found within approximately 100 miles of the coast in the Atlantic Coastal Plain.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in nutrient-poor, acidic sandy or peaty soils in open, sunny wetlands. The substrate remains consistently moist to wet year-round with fluctuating water tables. Associated with longleaf pine savannas, wet pocosins, and bog habitats with low-growing vegetation.

care.light: Full Sun or at least 20W per sq ft LED light. Venus flytraps love bright light and do best outdoors in full sun
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in 0.5”-1” of water using tray method. Flytraps can tolerate moist to soggy conditions, but do not allow the soil to dry out
care.soil: 1 part peat: 1 part sand, or 1 part peat: 1 part perlite. Pure long-fibered sphagnum moss is also good. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts
care.dormancy: Winter dormancy is necessary for long term health. Plants naturally go dormant in fall if grown outdoors. Throughout winter they will slow growth and shrink in size until spring. If growing indoors, gradually adjust photoperiod to around 9 hours in winter to induce dormancy. Reduce photoperiod and give cooler temperatures.
care.temp: Adaptable to any mild climate. Temperatures between 32-100F are best. Moderate humidity is fine. Avoid growing in enclosed areas without airflow. Winter dormancy is necessary for long term health
care.feeding: Feed indoor traps with fresh/hydrated bugs or insect slurry every month or so. Traps will occasionally reopen from inanimate, dry food — to help the trap you can very gently squeeze it a little to help stimulate digestion. Wait until leaves are fully developed for at least few days before feeding. Feed 25-50% of mature traps at a time to stimulate fast growth. Outdoor plants generally catch enough flies on their own.

If growing indoors, gradually adjust photoperiod to around 9 hours in winter to induce dormancy. Reduce photoperiod and give cooler temperatures.
care.temp: Adaptable to any mild climate. Temperatures between 32-100F are best. Moderate humidity is fine. Avoid growing in enclosed areas without airflow. Winter dormancy is necessary for long term health
care.feeding: Feed indoor traps with fresh/hydrated bugs or insect slurry every month or so. Traps will occasionally reopen from inanimate, dry food — to help the trap you can very gently squeeze it a little to help stimulate digestion. Wait until leaves are fully developed for at least few days before feeding. Feed 25-50% of mature traps at a time to stimulate fast growth. Outdoor plants generally catch enough flies on their own.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to Queensland, Australia. Found in a narrow coastal range from near Cooktown to Tully in northeastern Queensland.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows along shaded stream banks and seepage areas in tropical rainforest. The substrate is constantly moist to wet sandy or peaty soil with high organic content. Found at low elevations, typically below 300 meters, in deep shade with high humidity year-round.

care.temp: Temperatures between 50-90F are best. These prefer higher humidity and will be much dewier at moderate to high humidity. Unlike most plants, they love to be kept at max humidity if the soil is well aerated, like in a closed terrarium
care.light: Bright Shade or Indirect Sun, but these are great under lights at around 10W per square foot. They can also do ok at higher or lower light. If the leaves are overly red and look sunburned, the light is too high. If tentacles are green and leaves lack dew, it might need more light
care.water: These like to be moist, but not overly soggy. Don’t allow them to dry out. They can be grown using shallow trays. Only use distilled, reverse osmosis, or rainwater because tap water is toxic to carnivorous plants due to high mineral content and chlorine
care.soil: Soil can be 50% peat: 50% perlite, though most peat or sphagnum based mixes will be good. More drainage is helpful if the humidity is very high

taxa.nativeRange: Hybrid cultivar likely derived from subtropical or tropical Australian Drosera species.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Parent species typically grow in nutrient-poor, acidic substrates in wetland or seepage habitats. Substrate conditions vary but generally include sandy or peaty soils with consistent moisture. Found in open to partially shaded environments with high humidity.

care.light: Part to Full Sun or 20W per sq ft LED light. Does best outdoors or under powerful LEDs, but is adaptable
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in 0.5”-2” of water using tray method. Drosera capensis can tolerate very wet conditions perfectly fine if given bright light. Only use distilled, reverse osmosis, or rainwater because tap water is toxic to carnivorous plants due to high mineral content and chlorine
care.soil: 50% Peat Moss: 50% Perlite. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts
care.temp: Temperatures from 40-95F, all humidity levels. These will naturally slow growth in winter, and can go fully dormant if cold enough. Frost may cause them to die back to the roots

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to Western Australia, with records from the Albany region, including Lake Sepping, Princess Royal Harbour, Frenchman Bay, and Little Grove.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in dense shrub amongst reeds near paperbark trees, generally in black sandy soils on flats covered with water in winter.

care.temp: Mediterranean (SW Australia). 40-100F, low to moderate humidity.
care.light: Full Sun or 20W per sq ft LED light. Pygmy sundews love bright light.
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in 0.5”-1” of water using tray method.
care.soil: 40% Peat: 60% Perlite/Sand is a good mix. Pygmy Sundews like well draining soils. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts.
care.feeding: Feed leaves with Insect Powder every 2-4 weeks. Add an addon gemmae kit (Pygmy Sundew Soil + Pot + Tray) to combine with these gemmae for everything you need to get started. Just add water and light!

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to the Sierra Madre Oriental in Hidalgo and adjacent states of central Mexico. Found at elevations of 1,900-2,300 meters.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows on vertical limestone cliffs and rocky outcrops with constant seepage water and fog exposure. The substrate consists of thin moss layers over calcareous rock with excellent drainage. Found in cloud forest and pine-oak forest zones with very high humidity.

care.temp: Temperatures from 50-85F - they prefer some humidity but can adapt to lower humidity.
care.light: Part Sun or 15W per sq ft LED light. These do well under LED lights and will attain great color.
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in ~0.5” of water using tray method. They can also be top watered regularly in humid environments, but the tray method is simpler.
care.soil: Using standard garden store materials, a mix of roughly 20% Peat Moss: 50% Perlite: 30% Vermiculite will work. I like using a mix of Perlite/Volcanic Clay Granules/Vermiculite but lots of granular, well draining mixes will work. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts. Because the roots are shallow, they do not require deep pots but pot height will influence moisture wicking to the surface.
care.feeding: Dust the leaves with Insect Powder every 2-4 weeks to stimulate explosive growth.  Foliar feeding is also good at a frequency of once or twice per month with 1/4 tsp per gallon MaxSea or roughly 500ppm Dynagro Gro/Foliage Pro or Schultz Cactus Fertilizer.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to Western Australia, with records from the Regan's Ford (Moore River) and Boonanarring areas.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in well-drained, deep yellow sand between and under low shrubs on open heathland or under the shelter of Banksia menziesii woodland.

care.temp: Mediterranean (SW Australia). 40-100F, low to moderate humidity.
care.light: Full Sun or 20W per sq ft LED light. Pygmy sundews love bright light.
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in 0.5”-1” of water using tray method.

care.dormancy: Adjust photoperiod to 9-10 hours in winter, gradually cycling to 14-15 hours in summer. Proper lighting cues required to produce gemmae and flower in spring.
care.notes: WINTER SHIPPING: Cold winter temperatures (<40F) require heat packs which will be automatically included if necessary. Product is gemmae (10+ tiny modified leaves), not fully grown plants. Place on moist soil surface and keep humid; will root within ~1 week and reach flowering size in several months. Acclimate gradually from high to lower humidity over weeks using baggie method with progressive corner opening. Gemmae require strong light even at this stage.

taxa.nativeRange: Distributed across two regions in Western Australia: from Eneabba to York, and the Dumbleyung, Lake Grace, and Jilakin Rocks area.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows only on the summits of laterite hills in white silica sand and laterite gravel soils.

care.temp: Mediterranean (SW Australia). 40-100F, low to moderate humidity.
care.light: Full Sun or 20W per sq ft LED light. Pygmy sundews love bright light.
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in 0.5”-1” of water using tray method. Drosera barbigera prefers better drainage and less soggy conditions than other pygmies.
care.soil: 40% Peat: 60% Perlite/Sand is a good mix. Pygmy Sundews like well draining soils. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts. Drosera barbigera prefers more drainage than other pygmies so I would recommend a higher percentage of sand/perlite in the mix and deep pots.
care.feeding: Feed leaves with Insect Powder every 2-4 weeks. Add an addon gemmae kit (Pygmy Sundew Soil + Pot + Tray) to combine with these gemmae for everything you need to get started. Just add water and light!
care.dormancy: Adjust photoperiod to 9-10 hours in winter, gradually cycling to 14-15 hours in summer. Proper lighting cues required to produce gemmae and to flower in spring.
care.notes: Produces gemmae (tiny modified leaves) in fall that can be detached and grown into new plants much faster than seeds. Winter shipping includes heat packs if temperatures below 40F. Gemmae should be placed on moist soil surface and kept humid; will root within about a week and reach flowering size in several months. When growing gemmae, use high humidity initially (baggie method recommended) then gradually acclimate to lower humidity over weeks. Prefers better drainage and less soggy conditions than other pygmy sundews.

care.feeding: Feed leaves with Insect Powder every 2-4 weeks. Add an addon gemmae kit (Pygmy Sundew Soil + Pot + Tray) to combine with these gemmae for everything you need to get started. Just add water and light!

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to the Western Cape province of South Africa, found in the southwestern corner near Cape Town and surrounding regions.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in wet sandy or peaty soils in lowland fynbos and coastal wetlands. Occurs in seasonally wet depressions and seepage areas that remain moist year-round. Found at low elevations in Mediterranean climate regions with winter rainfall.

care.light: Part to Full Sun or 20W per sq ft LED light
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in 1” of water using tray method. Only use distilled, reverse osmosis, or rainwater because tap water is toxic to carnivorous plants due to high mineral content and chlorine. Seems to tolerate quite wet conditions perfectly fine
care.soil: 50% Peat Moss: 50% Perlite/Sand. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts
care.temp: Temperatures from 50-100F, low - moderate humidity. It seems to like good airflow

taxa.nativeRange: Widespread across Australia, Southeast Asia, and Pacific islands. Jardine River is in far northern Queensland, Australia, near Cape York Peninsula.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in sandy or peaty soils in wetlands, seepages, and along streams. Tolerates a wide range of conditions from coastal swamps to montane bogs. Found from sea level to over 2,000 meters in wet, sunny habitats.

care.light: Part to Full Sun or 20W per sq ft LED light
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in 0.5”-1” of water using tray method. Only use distilled, reverse osmosis, or rainwater because tap water is toxic to carnivorous plants due to high mineral content and chlorine
care.soil: 50% Peat Moss: 50% Perlite/Sand. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts
care.temp: Temperatures from 50-100F, all humidity levels

taxa.nativeRange: Native to mainland Southeast Asia, primarily Thailand, Cambodia, and southern Vietnam. Occurs in lowland regions from sea level to approximately 300 meters elevation.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in seasonally wet grasslands, open savannas, and sandy areas with periodic flooding during monsoon season. Found in nutrient-poor, acidic soils with fluctuating water tables that experience distinct wet and dry seasons. Associated vegetation includes grasses, sedges, and scattered shrubs in open, sun-exposed habitats.

care.light: Part to Full Sun or 20W per sq ft LED light
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in 0.5-1” of water using tray method. Only use distilled, reverse osmosis, or rainwater because tap water is toxic to carnivorous plants due to high mineral content and chlorine
care.soil: 30% Peat Moss: 70% Perlite/Sand. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts. I have grown it successfully in water trays with a very sandy mix, but have not experimented with other methods
care.temp: Temperatures from 50-100F, moderate humidity. It seems to like good airflow

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to the Western Cape province of South Africa. Betty's Bay is a coastal location southeast of Cape Town.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in wet sandy or peaty soils in coastal fynbos and sandplain wetlands. Occurs in seasonally moist depressions and seepage areas with winter rainfall. Found at low elevations in Mediterranean climate regions.

care.temp: Temperatures of 60-95F at all humidity levels.
care.light: Part Sun or at least 10W-15W per sq ft LED light. This species can tolerate brighter light and seems to be fine.
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Keep the soil constantly wet and do not allow it to dry out. This species does best for me with good drainage and shallow trays.
care.soil: 50% Peat Moss: 50% Sand/Perlite. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts.
care.feeding: Feed with MaxSea or Dynagro fertilizer diluted to 1/4 tsp per gallon (or roughly 200-400ppm) every month. Lightly spray the leaves but do not overfeed, especially in undrained containers.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to Queensland, Australia, found in coastal and near-coastal areas of the southeastern part of the state.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in wet sandy or peaty soils in coastal heathlands, wallum, and swampy areas. Occurs in acidic, nutrient-poor substrates with high water tables. Found at low elevations in subtropical coastal wetlands.

care.light: Part to Full Sun or 20W per sq ft LED light
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in 0.5”-1” of water using tray method. Only use distilled, reverse osmosis, or rainwater because tap water is toxic to carnivorous plants due to high mineral content and chlorine
care.soil: 50% Peat Moss: 50% Perlite/Sand. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts
care.temp: Temperatures from 40-95F, all humidity levels

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to the Northern Territory, Australia, particularly in the Darwin and Arnhem Land regions.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in shallow seasonal wetlands, sedgelands, and on the margins of billabongs and waterholes. Occurs in sandy or sandy-loam soils that are inundated during the wet season (November-April) and dry out during the dry season. Associated with tropical savanna vegetation including sedges, grasses, and scattered eucalypts.
taxa.provenanceWarning: This is an unregistered form of Byblis aquatica. Parent species B. aquatica is relatively well-documented but geographically restricted. Data provided is for the parent species native range and habitat.

care.temp: Temperatures from 60-100F, prefers high humidity but can adapt to lower humidity. These love heat and grow incredibly fast when kept hot
care.light: Part Sun or 20W per sq ft LED light
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in at least 2” of water using tray method
care.soil: 40% Peat Moss: 40% Sand: 20% Perlite or other well draining peat + sand mixes. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts
care.feeding: Feed regularly with insect powder or dilute liquid fertilizer.

taxa.notes: Short-lived annual that completes lifecycle in 4-6 months. 'Fine form' refers to more delicate, narrow leaves compared to typical form. Seeds require light and warmth to germinate; surface sow on moist media.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to the Kimberley region of northern Western Australia. Known from a limited area in the northern Kimberley.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in sandy soils in seasonally wet areas, often near seepage zones or along drainage lines. Occurs in open tropical woodland and savanna habitats with Eucalyptus and Melaleuca species. Found at low elevations in areas subject to a pronounced wet-dry seasonal pattern.

care.temp: Temperatures from 60-100F. These prefer moderate humidity but can easily acclimate to low humidity
care.light: Part to Full Sun or 20W per sq ft LED light. Plant in the sun will be more colorful, shorter, and more robust. Plants in less light will grow more like a vine
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in 0.5”-2” of water using tray method
care.soil: 40% Peat Moss: 40% Sand: 20% Perlite or other well draining peat + sand mixes. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts

taxa.displayName: Drosera aff. lanata (Flying Fox Creek, Northern Territory)
taxa.nativeRange: Known only from sandy habitats to the southeast of Darwin, Northern Territory.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in sandy soils. In the wet season has few hairs on its leaves; by the dry season the same leaves become densely hairy, similar to Drosera lanata.

care.light: Part to Full Sun or 20W per sq ft LED light
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in 0.5”-1” of water using the tray method (depending on pot height). Some species like very wet conditions, while others prefer good drainage and less moisture. This roughly correlates to how hairy they are (hairier = drier). However, most are quite tolerant of wet conditions if given high temperatures and powerful light. If plants decide to go dormant (shrink and slow growth), keep them drier until they resume growth
care.soil: I am using 25% Peat: 30% Perlite: 45% Coarse Sand for the hairier species, 40% Peat: 40% Sand: 20% Perlite for the wetter growing species. Soak and rinse out the media before use as these species do not like minerals/humic acids. The exact mix is not as important as the resulting moisture levels in the pot which will vary based on watering
care.temp: Temperatures from 70-100F, moderate to high humidity levels. Hairier species can acclimate to less humidity

taxa.nativeRange: An undescribed perennial tropical Drosera known from the Northern Territory.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in tropical savannah habitats of the Northern Territory. Distinguished from the Queensland Drosera petiolaris by its leaves up to 12 cm long and semi-erect pedicels 3–8 mm long.

care.light: Part to Full Sun or 20W per sq ft LED light
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in 0.5”-1” of water using the tray method (depending on pot height). Some species like very wet conditions, while others prefer good drainage and less moisture. This roughly correlates to how hairy they are (hairier = drier). However, most are quite tolerant of wet conditions if given high temperatures and powerful light. If plants decide to go dormant (shrink and slow growth), keep them drier until they resume growth. This form prefers wetter conditions but is quite adaptable
care.soil: I am using 25% Peat: 30% Perlite: 45% Coarse Sand for the hairier species, 40% Peat: 40% Sand: 20% Perlite for the wetter growing species. Soak and rinse out the media before use as these species do not like minerals/humic acids. The exact mix is not as important as the resulting moisture levels in the pot which will vary based on watering and pot height
care.temp: Temperatures from 70-100F, moderate to high humidity levels. Hairier species can acclimate to less humidity

taxa.species: aff. lanata "grey-green leaves, red traps, NT form" x paradoxa "Type Form"

taxa.nativeRange: Hybrid form from Theda in the Kimberley region of Western Australia, combining parent species from northern Australia.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Parent species occupy seasonally wet sandy soils in tropical savanna wetlands. These habitats experience extreme seasonal variation with monsoonal wet seasons and prolonged dry periods, in open sunny locations near creek lines and seepage areas.

care.temp: Temperatures from 50-100F, all humidity levels.
care.light: Part to Full Sun or 20W per sq ft LED light.
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in 0.5”-1” of water using tray method. Only use distilled, reverse osmosis, or rainwater because tap water is toxic to carnivorous plants due to high mineral content and chlorine.
care.soil: 50% Peat Moss: 50% Perlite/Sand. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts.
care.feeding: Feed leaves with Insect Powder every month or so.

taxa.nativeRange: Western Cape Province of South Africa, with this form from Betty's Bay on the southwestern coast.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in constantly moist acidic sandy soils in coastal fynbos vegetation and seepage areas. Plants occur from near sea level to low elevations in areas with year-round moisture from streams, seeps, or winter rainfall.

care.light: Part to Full Sun or 20W per sq ft LED light. Does best outdoors or under powerful LEDs, but is adaptable
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in 0.5”-2” of water using tray method. Drosera capensis can tolerate very wet conditions perfectly fine if given bright light. Only use distilled, reverse osmosis, or rainwater because tap water is toxic to carnivorous plants due to high mineral content and chlorine
care.soil: 50% Peat Moss: 50% Perlite. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts
care.temp: Temperatures from 40-100F, all humidity levels. These will naturally slow growth in winter, and can go fully dormant if cold enough. Frost may cause them to die back to the roots

taxa.nativeRange: Eastern Australia from Queensland through New South Wales to Victoria and Tasmania, also found in New Zealand.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Occupies permanently moist to waterlogged acidic sandy or peaty soils in coastal and montane wetlands, bogs, and streamside seepages. Found from near sea level to subalpine elevations in open sunny habitats with consistent moisture year-round.

care.temp: Temperatures from 50-100F, all humidity levels.
care.light: Part to Full Sun or 20W per sq ft LED light.
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in 0.5”-1” of water using tray method. Only use distilled, reverse osmosis, or rainwater because tap water is toxic to carnivorous plants due to high mineral content and chlorine.
care.soil: 50% Peat Moss: 50% Perlite/Sand. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts.
care.feeding: Feed leaves with Insect Powder every month or so.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to the Sierra Madre Oriental of Mexico, primarily in Hidalgo state at moderate to high elevations.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows on vertical limestone cliffs and rock faces in cloud forest zones. Plants root in thin moss layers and rock crevices with constant moisture from fog and mist, at elevations typically between 1,800-2,400 meters.

care.temp: Temperatures from 50-85F - they prefer some humidity but can adapt to lower humidity.
care.light: Part Sun or 15W per sq ft LED light. These do well under LED lights and will attain great color.
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in ~0.5” of water using tray method. They can also be top watered regularly in humid environments, but the tray method is simpler.
care.soil: Using standard garden store materials, a mix of roughly 20% Peat Moss: 50% Perlite: 30% Vermiculite will work. I like using a mix of Perlite/Volcanic Clay Granules/Vermiculite but lots of granular, well draining mixes will work. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts. Because the roots are shallow, they do not require deep pots but pot height will influence moisture wicking to the surface.
care.feeding: Dust the leaves with Insect Powder every 2-4 weeks to stimulate explosive growth.  Foliar feeding is also good at a frequency of once or twice per month with 1/4 tsp per gallon MaxSea or roughly 500ppm Dynagro Gro/Foliage Pro or Schultz Cactus Fertilizer.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to Nuevo León state in northeastern Mexico, with this population from near Aramberri in the Sierra Madre Oriental.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Inhabits vertical limestone cliffs and rock faces in pine-oak forest and cloud forest zones. Plants grow in moss and thin organic matter on limestone at elevations around 1,500-2,200 meters with high humidity and seasonal rainfall.

I like using a mix of Perlite/Volcanic Clay Granules/Vermiculite but lots of granular, well draining mixes will work. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts. Because the roots are shallow, they do not require deep pots but pot height will influence moisture wicking to the surface.

taxa.nativeRange: Hybrid of two Mexican species from the Sierra Madre Oriental, both endemic to central-eastern Mexico at moderate elevations.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Parent species occupy limestone cliffs and rock faces in cloud forest and pine-oak forest zones. These habitats feature vertical surfaces with moss and thin soils, high humidity, and elevations typically between 1,500-2,500 meters.

care.temp: Temperatures from 50-85F - they prefer some humidity but can adapt to lower humidity.
care.light: Part Sun or 15W per sq ft LED light. These do well under LED lights and will attain great color.
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in ~0.5” of water using tray method. They can also be top watered regularly in humid environments, but the tray method is simpler.
care.soil: Using standard garden store materials, a mix of roughly 20% Peat Moss: 50% Perlite: 30% Vermiculite will work. I like using a mix of Perlite/Volcanic Clay Granules/Vermiculite but lots of granular, well draining mixes will work. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts. Because the roots are shallow, they do not require deep pots but pot height will influence moisture wicking to the surface. Feeding : Dust the leaves with Insect Powder every 2-4 weeks to stimulate explosive growth. Foliar feeding is also good at a frequency of once or twice per month with 1/4 tsp per gallon MaxSea or roughly 500ppm Dynagro Gro/Foliage Pro or Schultz Cactus Fertilizer.
care.dormancy: This hybrid does not have a distinct winter dormancy but may shrink in size briefly. Plants ordered in winter/early spring may be in their succulent phase with dense rosette of small succulent leaves; sizing is based on carnivorous size of previous season.
care.notes: Tropical Pinguicula ship bareroot and need to be planted upon arrival. Tropical Pinguicula soil mix is available in the shop. Cold winter temperatures (<40F) require heat packs which will be automatically included if necessary. During winter and early spring, plants may appear smaller during their winter succulent phase and will grow new large carnivorous leaves in spring.

taxa.nativeRange: Widespread across northern Australia, occurring in the Northern Territory, Queensland, and Western Australia.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in seasonally wet to waterlogged sandy soils in tropical savanna wetlands and swamps. Plants occupy shallow depressions and margins of seasonal pools that fill during the wet season, typically in full sun exposure.

care.notes: Winter shipping: Cold winter temperatures (<40F) require heat packs which will be automatically included with the shipment if necessary. Plants are potted in 2.5"x 2.5"x 3.5" pots and will be flowering sized, but may or may not be flowering at the moment.

taxa.displayName: Pinguicula gypsicola (Buena Vista, San Luis Potosí)
taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to San Luis Potosí state in central Mexico, with this population from near Buena Vista in gypsum-rich habitats.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows on gypsum cliffs and outcrops in semi-arid highland regions. Plants root in thin soils and moss on gypsum substrates at elevations around 1,400-2,000 meters with seasonal rainfall and winter dormancy.

care.temp: Temperatures from 50-85F - they prefer some humidity but can adapt to lower humidity.
care.light: Part Sun or 15W per sq ft LED light. These do well under LED lights and will attain great color.
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in ~0.5” of water using tray method. They can also be top watered regularly in humid environments, but the tray method is simpler.  This species appreciates good drainage.  During winter dormancy keep this species quite dry.
care.soil: P. gypsicola seems to prefer alkaline, inorganic soils.  I use a mix of perlite, volcanic granules, and gypsum, though a more standard mix of perlite, volcanic granules, and vermiculite might work.
care.feeding: Dust the leaves with Insect Powder every 2-4 weeks to stimulate explosive growth.  Foliar feeding is also good at a frequency of once or twice per month with 1/4 tsp per gallon MaxSea or roughly 500ppm Dynagro Gro/Foliage Pro or Schultz Cactus Fertilizer.
care.dormancy: Dry winter dormancy period during cool, dry season. Plant shrinks into succulent form with rounded, flat non-carnivorous leaves (completely different appearance than growing phase). Remains in dormant form with little growth for months before resuming active growth in warmer wet season with larger carnivorous leaves.
care.notes: Ships bareroot and must be potted upon arrival using Tropical Pinguicula soil mix (available separately). During winter/early spring, plants may arrive in winter succulent phase with smaller, thicker leaves. Heat packs automatically included if winter temperatures below 40F expected. Plant sizing based on carnivorous leaf size from summer/fall.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to central-eastern Mexico in the Sierra Madre Oriental, primarily in Hidalgo and adjacent states.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Inhabits vertical limestone cliffs and rock faces in cloud forest and pine-oak forest zones. Plants grow in moss mats and thin organic matter on limestone at elevations between 1,700-2,500 meters with high humidity from fog and mist.

care.temp: Temperatures from 50-85F - they prefer some humidity but can adapt to lower humidity.
care.light: Part Sun or 15W per sq ft LED light. These do well under LED lights and will attain great color.
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in ~1” of water using tray method. They can also be top watered regularly in humid environments, but the tray method is simpler. This species can tolerate wetter conditions quite well.
care.soil: Using standard garden store materials, a mix of roughly 20% Peat Moss: 50% Perlite: 30% Vermiculite will work. I like using a mix of Perlite/Volcanic Clay Granules/Vermiculite but lots of granular, well draining mixes will work. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts. Because the roots are shallow, they do not require deep pots but pot height will influence moisture wicking to the surface.
care.feeding: Dust the leaves with Insect Powder every 2-4 weeks to stimulate explosive growth.  Foliar feeding is also good at a frequency of once or twice per month with 1/4 tsp per gallon MaxSea or roughly 500ppm Dynagro Gro/Foliage Pro or Schultz Cactus Fertilizer.
care.dormancy: No dormancy requirement. However, many Tropical Butterworts including this species may enter a winter succulent phase during cool, dry winters where leaves shrink into a dense geometric cluster of non-carnivorous leaves with little growth for months, then burst out with larger carnivorous leaves in warmer wet season. During winter and early spring, plants may arrive in this winter succulent phase with smaller, thicker leaves.
care.notes: Tropical Pinguicula ship bareroot and need to be planted upon arrival; Pinguicula moctezumae may ship potted. Tropical Pinguicula soil mix available in shop. Cold winter temperatures (<40F) require heat packs which will be automatically included if necessary. Sizing is based on carnivorous leaf size from summer/fall.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to Nuevo León state in northeastern Mexico, occurring in the Sierra Madre Oriental at moderate elevations.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows on limestone cliffs and rock faces in oak-pine forest zones. Plants establish in moss and thin soils on vertical or near-vertical limestone surfaces at elevations around 1,500-2,200 meters with seasonal moisture.

care.light: Part Sun or 15W per sq ft LED light. These do well under LED lights and will attain great color.

I like using a mix of Perlite/Volcanic Clay Granules/Vermiculite but lots of granular, well draining mixes will work. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts. Because the roots are shallow, they do not require deep pots but pot height will influence moisture wicking to the surface.

taxa.nativeRange: Southwestern Australia, primarily in Western Australia. The parent species D. microscapa and D. pygmaea are both endemic to the southwest botanical province.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in seasonally wet sandy or lateritic soils in winter-wet depressions and seepage areas. Habitat is characterized by low heathland and open scrubland with associated Mediterranean-climate vegetation. Plants typically occur in shallow sandy substrates that remain moist during the winter growing season and dry out in summer.

care.temp: Mediterranean (SW Australia). 40-100F, low to moderate humidity.
care.light: Full Sun or 20W per sq ft LED light. Pygmy sundews love bright light.
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in 0.5”-1” of water using tray method.
care.soil: 40% Peat: 60% Perlite/Sand is a good mix. Pygmy Sundews like well draining soils. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts.
care.feeding: Feed leaves with Insect Powder every 2-4 weeks. Add an addon gemmae kit (Pygmy Sundew Soil + Pot + Tray) to combine with these gemmae for everything you need to get started. Just add water and light!

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to Western Australia, with a restricted range found only between Muchea and Wannamal, over a distance of approximately 40 km.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in heavy laterite soils.

care.temp: Mediterranean (SW Australia). 40-100F, low to moderate humidity.
care.light: Full Sun or 20W per sq ft LED light. Pygmy sundews love bright light.
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in 0.5”-1” of water using tray method.
care.soil: I use a more granular mix for D. bindoon compared to other pygmies. My mix includes high levels of akadama, perlite, (and/or sand) with some peat.
care.feeding: Feed leaves with Insect Powder every 2-4 weeks. Add an addon gemmae kit (Pygmy Sundew Soil + Pot + Tray) to combine with these gemmae for everything you need to get started. Just add water and light!
care.dormancy: Adjust photoperiod to 9-10 hours in winter, gradually cycling to 14-15 hours in summer. Proper lighting cues required to produce gemmae and flower in spring.
care.notes: Roots grow rapidly (>6") so deep pots recommended. Medium-sized gemmae producer. Gemmae should be placed on moist soil surface and kept humid; they will root within about a week and reach flowering size in several months. Acclimate gemmae from high to lower humidity gradually using baggie method over several weeks. Cold winter temperatures (<40F) require heat packs for shipping.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to Western Australia, with records from Avon Valley, North Bannister, and Boddington.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in Jarrah woodland habitat in open areas associated with jarrah forest.

care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in 0.5”-1” of water using tray method.

care.dormancy: Adjust photoperiod to 9-10 hours in winter, gradually cycling to 14-15 hours in summer. Proper lighting cues required to produce gemmae and flower in spring.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to Western Australia, with records from Gleneagle, Boulder Rock, and towards Jarrahdale.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows at scattered locations in Jarrah forest on loam soils, in peaty sand on winter-wet watersheds, and in loamy sand on the aprons of granite outcrops.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to Brazil, occurring in the eastern and central regions including Bahia, Minas Gerais, and surrounding states.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Inhabits seasonally wet sandy soils in campo rupestre and cerrado grasslands. Plants grow in shallow depressions and seepage areas that are saturated during the rainy season, typically at elevations from lowlands to moderate highlands.

care.temp: Temperatures of 60-95F at all humidity levels.
care.light: Part Sun or at least 10W-15W per sq ft LED light. This species can tolerate brighter light and seems to be fine.
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Keep the soil constantly wet and do not allow it to dry out. This species does best for me with good drainage and shallow trays.
care.soil: 50% Peat Moss: 50% Sand/Perlite. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts.
care.feeding: Feed with MaxSea or Dynagro fertilizer diluted to 1/4 tsp per gallon (or roughly 200-400ppm) every month. Lightly spray the leaves but do not overfeed, especially in undrained containers.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to Oaxaca state in southern Mexico, found in the Sierra Madre del Sur mountain range.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows on wet vertical limestone cliffs and rock faces with constant seepage. Found at elevations between 2,000-2,800 meters in cloud forest zones with high humidity and frequent fog.

care.temp: Temperatures from 50-85F - they prefer some humidity but can adapt to lower humidity.
care.light: Part Sun or 15W per sq ft LED light. These do well under LED lights and will attain great color.
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in ~0.5” of water using tray method. They can also be top watered regularly in humid environments, but the tray method is simpler.
care.soil: Using standard garden store materials, a mix of roughly 20% Peat Moss: 50% Perlite: 30% Vermiculite will work. I like using a mix of Perlite/Volcanic Clay Granules/Vermiculite but lots of granular, well draining mixes will work. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts. Because the roots are shallow, they do not require deep pots but pot height will influence moisture wicking to the surface.
care.feeding: Dust the leaves with Insect Powder every 2-4 weeks to stimulate explosive growth. Foliar feeding is also good at a frequency of once or twice per month with 1/4 tsp per gallon MaxSea or roughly 500ppm Dynagro Gro/Foliage Pro or Schultz Cactus Fertilizer.
care.dormancy: Does not truly go dormant but shrinks in size during winter while remaining carnivorous. During cool, dry winters, plants enter a winter succulent phase with dense, geometric clusters of non-carnivorous succulent leaves, remaining in this form with little growth for months before resuming larger, carnivorous leaves in warmer wet season.
care.notes: Tropical Pinguicula ship bareroot and must be planted upon arrival. Plants ordered in winter/early spring may be in succulent phase appearing small, but will resume substantially larger carnivorous growth. Tropical Pinguicula soil mix available for purchase. Winter shipping below 40°F includes heat packs. Sizing is based on carnivorous stage from previous season.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to coastal areas of North and South Carolina in the southeastern United States, within approximately 100 miles of Wilmington, North Carolina.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in nutrient-poor, acidic soils of pine savannas and pocosins with seasonal waterlogged conditions. Requires periodic fire disturbance and full sun in sandy or peaty wetlands near the coastal plain.

care.temp: Adaptable to any mild climate. Temperatures between 32-100F are best. Moderate humidity is fine. Avoid growing in enclosed areas without airflow. Winter dormancy is necessary for long term health. Reduce photoperiod and give cooler temperatures.
care.light: Full Sun or at least 20W per sq ft LED light. Venus flytraps love bright light and do best outdoors in full sun.
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in 0.5”-1” of water using tray method. Flytraps can tolerate moist to soggy conditions, but do not allow the soil to dry out.
care.soil: 1 part peat: 1 part sand, or 1 part peat: 1 part perlite. Pure long-fibered sphagnum moss is also good. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts.
care.feeding: Feed indoor traps with fresh/hydrated bugs or insect slurry every month or so. Traps will occasionally reopen from inanimate, dry food — to help the trap you can very gently squeeze it a little to help stimulate digestion. Wait until leaves are fully developed for at least few days before feeding. Feed 25-50% of mature traps at a time to stimulate fast growth. Outdoor plants generally catch enough flies on their own.
care.dormancy: Winter dormancy is necessary for long term health. Plants naturally go dormant in fall if grown outdoors. Throughout winter they will slow growth and shrink in size until spring. If growing indoors, gradually adjust photoperiod to around 9 hours in winter to induce dormancy. Reduce photoperiod and give cooler temperatures.
care.notes: Plants ordered in winter or early spring may still be coming out of dormancy and can appear slightly smaller; they will quickly grow many new, large leaves as spring progresses. If dormant, sizing is based on previous year summer size. For smaller plants, it can be helpful to cut off the flower stalk to save energy; the cut stalk can be planted in soil to grow a new plant.

care.temp: Mediterranean (SW Australia). 40-100F, low to moderate humidity.
care.light: Full Sun or 20W per sq ft LED light. Pygmy sundews love bright light.
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in 0.5”-1” of water using tray method.
care.soil: 40% Peat: 60% Perlite/Sand is a good mix. Pygmy Sundews like well draining soils. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts.
care.feeding: Feed leaves with Insect Powder every 2-4 weeks.
care.dormancy: Adjust photoperiod to 9-10 hours in winter, gradually cycling to 14-15 hours in summer. Proper lighting cues required to produce gemmae and flower in spring.
care.notes: Ships potted in 2.5"W x 3.5"H pots with multiple small plants (3+). Heat packs automatically included with winter shipments if temperatures below 40°F. Produces gemmae (tiny modified leaves) in fall that can detach and grow into new plants faster than from seed.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to Western Australia, with a restricted range found only between Muchea and Wannamal, over a distance of approximately 40 km.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in heavy laterite soils.

care.temp: Mediterranean (SW Australia). 40-100F, low to moderate humidity.
care.light: Full Sun or 20W per sq ft LED light. Pygmy sundews love bright light.
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in 0.5”-1” of water using tray method.
care.soil: I use a more granular mix for D. bindoon compared to other pygmies. My mix includes high levels of akadama, perlite, (and/or sand) with some peat.
care.feeding: Feed leaves with Insect Powder every 2-4 weeks.
care.dormancy: Adjust photoperiod to 9-10 hours in winter, gradually cycling to 14-15 hours in summer. Requires proper lighting cues to produce gemmae and to flower in spring.
care.notes: Ships potted in 2.5"W x 3.5"H pots with multiple small plants (3+). Roots grow rapidly and can exceed 6" in length, so deep pots are recommended. Produces medium-sized gemmae (tiny modified leaves) in fall that detach and grow into new plants much faster than from seed. Heat packs automatically included with shipment during winter if temperatures below 40F are expected.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to Western Australia, with records from Esperance to Cape Le Grand and Cape Arid.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in sandy soils in low, open heath in the coastal region east of Esperance.

care.temp: Mediterranean (SW Australia). 40-100F, low to moderate humidity.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to the northern parts of the Northern Territory, Australia, particularly in the Darwin and surrounding coastal regions.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Occurs in seasonally wet sandy soils and seepage areas in open eucalypt woodland and tropical savanna. Found in areas with strong seasonal rainfall patterns, typically in shallow sandy substrates over laterite or sandstone. Associated vegetation includes Eucalyptus species, Melaleuca, and sedges in open to moderately wooded habitats.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to southwestern Australia, occurring in a narrow coastal strip north of Perth in Western Australia, primarily between Eneabba and Badgingarra.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in deep white or grey sand in winter-wet depressions and seepage areas on sandplains. The habitat is characterized by seasonal waterlogging during the cool, wet winter months followed by well-drained conditions in summer. Associated vegetation includes low shrubland and heath communities dominated by Banksia, Eucalyptus, and other sclerophyllous plants typical of the Southwest Australian Floristic Region.

taxa.nativeRange: Found in northern Australia including the Northern Territory (Top End) and Queensland (Cape York Peninsula). Also occurs in southern Papua New Guinea.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in seasonally wet sandy soils in open tropical savanna habitats. Typically found on sandy flats, seepage areas, and margins of seasonal wetlands that become waterlogged during the wet season and dry out during the dry season. Often occurs with grasses, sedges, and other seasonal herbs at low elevations.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to coastal areas of North and South Carolina in the southeastern United States, within approximately 100 miles of Wilmington, North Carolina.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in nutrient-poor, acidic soils of pine savannas and pocosins with seasonal waterlogged conditions. Requires periodic fire disturbance and full sun in sandy or peaty wetlands near the coastal plain.

taxa.nativeRange: Native to the coastal plains of North and South Carolina in the United States, within a roughly 100-mile radius around Wilmington, North Carolina.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Found in wet, acidic, nutrient-poor sandy or peaty soils in pine savannas and pocosin wetlands. Grows in full sun where the water table is high but not permanently flooded, often with Sphagnum mosses and grasses. Occurs at low elevations near sea level.

taxa.nativeRange: Southeastern Brazil.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in campos rupestres montane grassland habitat.
taxa.provenanceWarning: Drosera ascendens is a poorly documented species with very limited information available in scientific literature. The species name appears in taxonomic lists but detailed ecological and distributional data are scarce. There is risk of confusion with other highland Drosera species from the same region.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to the state of Minas Gerais in southeastern Brazil, specifically in the Serra do Espinhaço mountain range. Known from campo rupestre habitats in the central and southern portions of the Espinhaço range.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in high-altitude rocky grasslands known as campo rupestre, typically on sandy or gravelly soils with quartzite and sandstone substrates. Found in open, sunny habitats with seasonal rainfall patterns, often in seepage areas or along streams where moisture is more consistently available. Associated vegetation includes other herbaceous plants, grasses, and dwarf shrubs adapted to nutrient-poor, well-drained soils at elevations between 900-1400 meters.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to the Serra do Grão Mogol in Minas Gerais state, Brazil. Known only from the type locality in the Grão Mogol mountain range.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in rocky campos rupestres (rupestrian grasslands) at high elevations, typically on quartzitic or sandy soils among rock outcrops. Found in open, exposed habitats with seasonally moist conditions and well-drained substrates. Associated with other specialized campo rupestre vegetation including grasses, eriocaulads, and xyridaceous plants at elevations around 1000-1400 meters.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to Minas Gerais state in southeastern Brazil. Known only from a single mountain in the Pico do Padre Ângelo region.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows on quartzitic and sandy soils on steep rock faces and outcrops at elevations around 1,500 meters. Found in areas with high humidity and frequent cloud cover on vertical to near-vertical rock surfaces. Associated with other rupestrian vegetation typical of Brazilian campo rupestre formations.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to the Guiana Highlands of northern South America, primarily on Mount Roraima and adjacent tepuis in Venezuela, Guyana, and northern Brazil (Roraima state). Also recorded from other tepuis in the region including Kukenán and Wei-Assipu.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows on exposed sandstone and quartzite substrates on tepui summits and slopes at elevations between 1,500 and 2,810 meters. Found in nutrient-poor, acidic soils with high moisture from frequent fog and rainfall, often in seepage areas and along streams. Associated with sparse vegetation including other carnivorous plants, grasses, sedges, and low shrubs characteristic of tepui ecosystems.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to Brazil, specifically found in the states of Minas Gerais and Bahia in the eastern part of the country.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in seasonally wet sandy soils on rocky outcrops and campos rupestres (rupestrian grasslands) at elevations typically between 900-1400 meters. The substrate is typically nutrient-poor quartzitic or sandy soils with good drainage during dry periods. Associated vegetation includes other herbaceous plants, grasses, and scrubby vegetation adapted to the alternating wet and dry seasons of the highland savanna ecosystem.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to Brazil, occurring in the states of Minas Gerais, São Paulo, and possibly adjacent areas of southeastern Brazil.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in campos rupestres (rocky grasslands) and campo cerrado habitats at elevations typically between 900-1400 meters. Found on sandy or rocky soils with quartzite substrates, often in seepage areas or moist depressions with low vegetation. Associated with other herbaceous plants and low shrubs in open, exposed sites with high light levels.

taxa.country: Australia, Papua New Guinea, Southeast Asia
taxa.nativeRange: Widespread across tropical northern Australia, Southeast Asia, southern China, and Papua New Guinea. The Humpty Doo form is from the Northern Territory, Australia.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows as an annual in seasonally wet soils of tropical grasslands and open areas. Typically found in sandy or lateritic soils that become waterlogged during the wet season and dry during the dry season. Occurs at low elevations in full sun.

taxa.nativeRange: Widespread across tropical and subtropical Africa including Madagascar, with populations in southern, eastern, and western Africa. The Okavango Delta population is from Botswana.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in seasonally wet sandy soils of grasslands, marshes, and open wetlands. Found in areas with pronounced wet and dry seasons, often in disturbed habitats. Occurs at low to moderate elevations in full sun exposure.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to Madagascar, found in central highlands and various regions of the island. Antsirabe is located in the central highlands of Madagascar.

care.dormancy: Adjust photoperiod to 9-10 hours in winter, gradually cycling to 14-15 hours in summer. Proper lighting cues required for gemmae production and spring flowering.
care.notes: Ships potted in 2.5"W x 3.5"H pots with multiple small plants (3+). Heat packs automatically included if winter temperatures below 40F during shipping.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to Western Australia, with records from the Stirling Range, Ravensthorpe, Hopetoun, Scaddan, Esperance, and Cape Arid.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in deep white sand, or laterite and silica sand soils, under and between low heath and open woodland in a coastal region.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to tropical northeast Queensland, Australia, found in a small area between Cooktown and Tully. Bishops Peak is within this range.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows along streams and creek margins in deep shade of tropical rainforest. Requires constantly moist to wet sandy or gravelly soils rich in organic matter, with high humidity and low light conditions. Found at low to moderate elevations typically below 600 meters.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to tropical northeast Queensland, Australia, in a restricted area near Cooktown and Cape Tribulation.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in deep shade along rainforest streams and seepage areas. Requires constantly wet sandy or peaty soils with high organic content, in conditions of high humidity and low light. Found at low elevations in tropical rainforest habitats.

taxa.displayName: Drosera capensis Drosera capensis Betty's Bay
taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to the Western Cape and Eastern Cape provinces of South Africa. Betty's Bay is located in the Western Cape along the coast.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in permanently moist sandy or peaty soils along stream margins, wetlands, and seepage areas in fynbos vegetation. Requires year-round moisture with winter rainfall patterns, in full sun to partial shade. Found from sea level to moderate elevations.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to the Western Cape Province of South Africa, primarily in the southwestern Cape region including the Cape Peninsula and surrounding mountains.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in sandy soils on rock sheets, seepage areas, and wet flats in fynbos vegetation. Requires seasonally moist to wet conditions with winter rainfall and summer dryness, typically in full sun. Found at low to moderate elevations in the Mediterranean climate zone.

taxa.displayName: Drosera cuneifolia Drosera cuneifolia var undulata (Silvermine)
taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to the Western Cape Province of South Africa. Silvermine is located on the Cape Peninsula near Cape Town.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in sandy soils on rock sheets, seepage areas, and wet flats in fynbos vegetation. Requires seasonally moist to wet conditions with winter rainfall and summer dryness, typically in full sun. Found at low to moderate elevations in the Mediterranean climate zone.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to southwestern Western Australia, found in a limited area near Albany and the Stirling Range.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in moist sandy or gravelly soils in winter-wet depressions and seepage areas. Occurs in kwongan heath vegetation with Mediterranean climate, requiring winter moisture and tolerating summer dryness. Found at low to moderate elevations in coastal and near-coastal areas.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to New Caledonia in the southwestern Pacific Ocean, found primarily on the main island Grande Terre.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in wet, nutrient-poor soils on ultramafic substrates rich in heavy metals. Inhabits boggy seepage areas and wet heathlands at mid to high elevations, typically between 500-1400 meters. Associated with sparse scrubland vegetation adapted to serpentine soils.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to the Western Cape Province of South Africa, occurring in the southwestern Cape region near coastal areas.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Found in seasonally wet seepage zones on sandstone-derived soils in fynbos vegetation. Grows in areas with winter rainfall and summer moisture from fog and seeps, typically at low to mid elevations. Plants form compact rosettes in sandy, nutrient-poor substrates.

taxa.nativeRange: Found on Lantau Island in Hong Kong, specifically documented from Sunset Peak (Tai Tung Shan) and surrounding high-elevation areas.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in wet, acidic soils on exposed hillsides and along seepage lines at elevations around 800-900 meters. Inhabits areas with year-round moisture from fog and rainfall, in poor, sandy or peaty substrates. Associated with low grassland and heath vegetation on mountain slopes.

taxa.nativeRange: Native to southern China, particularly in Guangdong Province in the Pearl River Delta region and adjacent areas.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Inhabits wet, sandy or peaty soils in open wetlands, seepage areas, and margins of rice paddies. Found at low elevations in subtropical wetlands with year-round warmth and high humidity. Grows in nutrient-poor, acidic substrates often associated with disturbed or semi-natural habitats.

taxa.nativeRange: Horticultural hybrid derived from South African winter-growing species, primarily D. nidiformis parentage.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Parent species grows in seasonally wet seepage zones on sandstone-derived soils in fynbos vegetation. Found in areas with winter rainfall and summer moisture from fog and seeps, typically at low to mid elevations. Plants form compact rosettes in sandy, nutrient-poor substrates.

taxa.nativeRange: Occurs in the southeastern United States from Virginia south to Florida and west to Texas, extending into northeastern Mexico.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Found in moist, sandy soils in open pine savannas, wet prairies, and roadside ditches. Grows as an annual or short-lived perennial in disturbed and natural wetlands at low elevations. Prefers sunny, seasonally wet habitats with acidic, nutrient-poor soils.

taxa.nativeRange: Horticultural hybrid derived from North American temperate species including D. brevifolia.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Parent species grows in moist, sandy soils in open pine savannas, wet prairies, and roadside ditches. Found as an annual or short-lived perennial in disturbed and natural wetlands at low elevations. Prefers sunny, seasonally wet habitats with acidic, nutrient-poor soils.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to Western Australia, found in the Stirling Range area northeast of Albany in the southwest botanical region.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in sandy or gravelly soils on slopes and flats in kwongan heathland vegetation. Found in seasonally moist to wet areas that dry out significantly in summer. Associated with low shrubland and heath communities typical of the southwest Australian floristic region.

taxa.nativeRange: South Australia – Bordertown, Glencoe, swamps between Mt Compass and Mt Moon, Naracoorte, Peters Creek, and Kangaroo Island. Victoria – Raymond Island, Middle Tarwin, Snake Island, San Remo, Wilsons Promontory, and Brisbane Ranges. New South Wales – Braidwood, Kempsey, Mulgoa, Nelligen, Nowra, Pigeon House Range, Yetholme, Ilford, and Bilpin. Tasmania – Bruny Island, between Deadman's Bay and Lousy Bay, Flinders Island, King Island, Mt Nelson, Corinna, and Ulverstone. New Zealand – throughout the North Island and the northern tip of the South Island.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in laterite and sandy clay in South Australia; grey sand in dry situations under Eucalyptus in Tasmania; and in open, poorly drained areas in moss pads on clay flats or in areas of regenerating bush, often at the base of tea tree, in New Zealand.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to the Western Cape Province of South Africa, widespread across the southwestern Cape winter rainfall region.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in heavy clay or sandy soils in fynbos, renosterveld, and coastal lowlands. Active during winter rainy season (May-October), dormant in summer as underground stem. Found from sea level to mid elevations, typically in areas with Mediterranean climate and winter precipitation.

taxa.nativeRange: Found in the Western Cape Province of South Africa, specifically from the Hopefield area in the Swartland region.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in heavy clay or sandy soils in renosterveld and fynbos vegetation with winter rainfall. Active during winter rainy season, dormant in summer as underground stem. Found in flats and gentle slopes at low elevations in areas with Mediterranean climate.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to the Western Cape Province of South Africa, occurring in the southwestern Cape winter rainfall region.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in heavy clay or sandy soils in fynbos and renosterveld vegetation. Active during winter rainy season (May-October), dormant in summer as underground stem. Found from coastal areas to inland valleys at low to mid elevations in Mediterranean climate zones.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to southwestern Western Australia, occurring in a limited range in the southern coastal and subcoastal regions of the southwest botanical province.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in winter-wet sandy or gravelly soils in tall open shrubland and forest margins. The tallest Drosera species, producing climbing stems up to 1 meter during the cool, wet winter season, then dying back to underground tubers in summer. Found in deep sandy soils on sandplains at low elevations.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to the Western Cape Province of South Africa, occurring in the southwestern Cape winter rainfall region near coastal areas.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in sandy soils in coastal fynbos and sandplain vegetation. Active during winter rainy season, forming basal rosettes that die back to underground tubers during dry summer months. Found at low elevations in areas with Mediterranean climate, often in white or grey sands.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to southeastern Australia, found in Tasmania and Victoria.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in wet heathlands, sedgelands, and buttongrass moorlands on sandy or peaty soils. Occurs in areas with seasonal waterlogging and high rainfall, typically at low to moderate elevations.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to southeastern Australia, found in Tasmania and Victoria. Lake Eildon is located in Victoria.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in wet heathlands, sedgelands, and buttongrass moorlands on sandy or peaty soils. Occurs in areas with seasonal waterlogging and high rainfall, typically at low to moderate elevations.

taxa.displayName: Drosera hookeri orange red (Conara Junction, Tasmania)
taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to southeastern Australia, found in Tasmania and Victoria. Conara Junction is in Tasmania.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in wet heathlands, sedgelands, and buttongrass moorlands on sandy or peaty soils. Occurs in areas with seasonal waterlogging and high rainfall, typically at low to moderate elevations.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to southwestern Western Australia. Holt Rock is located in the central wheatbelt region.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in sandy or gravelly soils in winter-wet depressions and seepage areas within heathland and low woodland. Occurs in habitats that are waterlogged in winter and dry in summer, at low elevations.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to southwestern Western Australia, primarily in the Perth region and coastal areas south to Albany.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Inhabits sandy soils in winter-wet swamps, seepage zones, and depressions in heathland and banksia woodland. Experiences seasonal inundation during winter rainfall period, completely dry in summer.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to southwestern Western Australia, widespread from Perth region south to Albany and east to Esperance.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in sandy or gravelly soils in winter-wet heathlands, swamps, and road verges. Occurs in seasonally waterlogged sites that dry out completely in summer, typically at low elevations in high rainfall areas.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to southwestern Western Australia, distributed from north of Perth south to Albany.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Inhabits sandy or lateritic soils in winter-wet swamps, seepage areas, and heathlands. Grows in sites with seasonal waterlogging during winter months, becoming dry in summer.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to southwestern Western Australia, restricted to a limited range in the Perth to Margaret River region.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in sandy soils in winter-wet depressions and seepage zones within heathland and low scrub. Occurs in seasonally inundated habitats at low elevations with Mediterranean-type climate.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to southwestern Western Australia, widespread across the region from Geraldton south to Albany.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in sandy or gravelly soils in winter-wet heathlands, swamps, and road verges. Inhabits seasonally waterlogged sites that are completely dry in summer, typically at low elevations.

taxa.nativeRange: Hybrid occurring in southwestern Western Australia where parent species ranges overlap.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in sandy or lateritic soils in winter-wet heathlands and seepage areas. Occurs in seasonally waterlogged habitats typical of Mediterranean climate regions.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to southwestern Western Australia, found in scattered populations in the central and southern wheatbelt and coastal areas.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Inhabits sandy or gravelly soils in winter-wet depressions, seepage zones, and heathlands. Grows in seasonally waterlogged sites that dry out in summer.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to southwestern Western Australia, distributed across the region from Perth south to Albany.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in sandy soils in winter-wet heathlands, swamps, and seepage areas. Occurs in seasonally waterlogged habitats at low elevations with Mediterranean climate.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to southwestern Western Australia, widespread from north of Perth south to Esperance.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Inhabits sandy or lateritic soils in winter-wet heathlands, swamps, and road verges. Grows in seasonally waterlogged sites that are dry in summer, at low elevations.
