taxa.nativeHabitat: Winter-growing tuberous sundews inhabiting sandy or gravelly soils in kwongan heathland. Plants grow during the wet winter months and retreat to underground tubers during hot, dry summers. Typically found in seasonal wetlands and sandy flats with winter inundation.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to southwestern Western Australia, occurring in coastal and near-coastal areas from Perth south to Albany and east to Esperance.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Winter-growing tuberous sundew found in white or grey sand in low-lying flats and depressions. Grows in seasonally wet heathland and scrubland that is inundated in winter but dries completely in summer. Often found in kwongan vegetation communities.

taxa.displayName: Drosera stolonifera Mattner x dark green form
taxa.nativeRange: Parent species Drosera stolonifera is endemic to southwestern Western Australia, occurring in coastal and near-coastal areas from Perth south to Albany and east to Esperance.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Winter-growing tuberous sundew found in white or grey sand in low-lying flats and depressions. Grows in seasonally wet heathland and scrubland that is inundated in winter but dries completely in summer. Often found in kwongan vegetation communities.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to the southwestern Cape region of South Africa, occurring from the Cape Peninsula eastward to the Agulhas Plain.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Winter-growing rosetted sundew inhabiting seasonally wet sandy soils in lowland fynbos. Found in seeps, vleis, and marshy areas that are saturated during winter rains but may dry somewhat in summer. Grows among low vegetation at elevations from sea level to approximately 1000 meters.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to the Western Cape Province of South Africa, specifically known from the Stormsvleikloof area in the Kogelberg region.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows on steep sandstone slopes and cliff faces in nutrient-poor, acidic sandy soils derived from Table Mountain Sandstone. Found in fynbos vegetation, typically in seepage areas where moisture is present year-round. Occurs at elevations between 300-800 meters in areas with high humidity and periodic mist.

taxa.nativeRange: Table Mountain form occurs on Table Mountain in the Cape Peninsula, southwestern Cape region of South Africa.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Winter-growing rosetted sundew inhabiting seasonally wet sandy soils in lowland fynbos. Found in seeps, vleis, and marshy areas that are saturated during winter rains but may dry somewhat in summer. Grows among low vegetation at elevations from sea level to approximately 1000 meters.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to southwestern Western Australia, occurring primarily in the Stirling Range and adjacent areas inland from Albany.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Winter-growing tuberous sundew found in sandy or gravelly soils in heathland and kwongan. Inhabits seasonally wet flats and slopes that receive winter rainfall and are dry in summer. Often grows in areas with shallow sandy soil over laterite or granite.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to southwestern Western Australia, occurring in the Yilgarn region and adjacent inland areas east of the Darling Scarp.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Winter-growing tuberous sundew inhabiting sandy or gravelly soils in inland heathland. Found in seasonally wet depressions and flats that receive winter rainfall. Grows in semi-arid regions where plants are dormant as underground tubers during the long, hot, dry summer.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to southwestern Western Australia, occurring in the Stirling Range and surrounding areas north of Albany.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Winter-growing tuberous sundew found in sandy or lateritic soils in heathland and woodland margins. Inhabits seasonally moist areas on slopes and flats with winter rainfall. Plants grow during wet winter months and retreat to underground tubers during summer drought.

taxa.nativeRange: Drosera zigzagia is endemic to southwestern Western Australia, occurring in the Stirling Range and surrounding areas north of Albany. This entry appears to contain an error as D. zigzagia does not occur in Spain.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Winter-growing tuberous sundew found in sandy or lateritic soils in heathland and woodland margins. Inhabits seasonally moist areas on slopes and flats with winter rainfall. Plants grow during wet winter months and retreat to underground tubers during summer drought.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to southwestern Western Australia, occurring from the Eneabba region south to Perth and inland areas of the northern Swan Coastal Plain.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Winter-growing tuberous sundew found in deep white or yellow sand in heathland. Inhabits sandy plains and rises with winter rainfall, often in areas underlain by laterite. One of the more drought-tolerant winter-growing species, occurring in semi-arid zones.

Drosera caduca wide leaf, white flower (Bachsten Creek, the Kimberley, Western Australia)
taxa.nativeRange: Distributed throughout the Edkins Range to the southern regions of the Prince Regent River Reserve. Also occurs on Augustus Island, Kimberley, Western Australia.
taxa.nativeHabitat: On the mainland, grows on creek margins in silty white sand soils. On Augustus Island, grows in stony brown sand on the edge of a basin on the lower slopes of a sandstone ridge with Eucalyptus miniata woodland and Spinifex.

taxa.nativeRange: Known from Western Australia. Common on the Yampi Peninsula and a number of offshore islands in the Buccaneer Archipelago to the northwest of the peninsula. Also extends south of the Yampi Peninsula to the Erskine Ranges and southeast to the Leopold Ranges.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in sandy soils on the aprons of sandstone rock outcrops, as well as in seasonally wet savannahs.

taxa.nativeRange: Occurs in the Northern Territory, with records from Palmerston, Berry Springs, Fly Creek, the Finniss River catchment, and Melville Island.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in grey, silty clay, often on gently sloping savannah in areas that are inundated for periods of time, especially during the earlier part of the wet season.

taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in sandy soil on damp flats, seepage areas, and ephemeral wet depressions just above the wet season flood levels. Plants may remain under water for several weeks at a time during heavy rains and flooding.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to Queensland, with records from Mareeba, Chewko, and Undarra.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in sandy or gravelly well-drained soils in woodlands or in shallow, decomposed granite in rocky areas, often in open Casuarina woodland. In the Mareeba region on the Atherton Tablelands, favours well-drained white sand habitats.

care.temp: Temperatures from 50-85F - they prefer some humidity but can adapt to lower humidity.
care.light: Part Sun or 15W per sq ft LED light. These do well under LED lights and will attain great color.
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in ~0.5” of water using tray method. They can also be top watered regularly in humid environments, but the tray method is simpler.
care.soil: Using standard garden store materials, a mix of roughly 20% Peat Moss: 50% Perlite: 30% Vermiculite will work. I like using a mix of Perlite/Volcanic Clay Granules/Vermiculite but lots of granular, well draining mixes will work. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts. Because the roots are shallow, they do not require deep pots but pot height will influence moisture wicking to the surface.
care.feeding: Dust the leaves with Insect Powder every 2-4 weeks to stimulate explosive growth.  Foliar feeding is also good at a frequency of once or twice per month with 1/4 tsp per gallon MaxSea or roughly 500ppm Dynagro Gro/Foliage Pro or Schultz Cactus Fertilizer.

taxa.nativeRange: Tropical Pinguicula species occur primarily in Mexico, with distributions extending through Guatemala and into the Caribbean islands.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Most Mexican Pinguicula grow on wet limestone cliffs, rocky outcrops, and moss-covered walls in cloud forests and pine-oak forests. They typically occur at elevations from 1000-3000 meters in areas with high humidity and seasonal rainfall patterns. Substrates are often calcareous with constant moisture from seepage or fog.

taxa.displayName: Pinguicula Hybrids Pinguicula moctezumae x rotundiflora Aramberri

care.temp: Temperatures from 50-85F - they prefer some humidity but can adapt to lower humidity.
care.light: Part Sun or 15W per sq ft LED light. These do well under LED lights and will attain great color.
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in ~0.5” of water using tray method. They can also be top watered regularly in humid environments, but the tray method is simpler.
care.soil: Using standard garden store materials, a mix of roughly 20% Peat Moss: 50% Perlite: 30% Vermiculite will work. I like using a mix of Perlite/Volcanic Clay Granules/Vermiculite but lots of granular, well draining mixes will work. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts. Because the roots are shallow, they do not require deep pots but pot height will influence moisture wicking to the surface.
care.feeding: Dust the leaves with Insect Powder every 2-4 weeks to stimulate explosive growth.  Foliar feeding is also good at a frequency of once or twice per month with 1/4 tsp per gallon MaxSea or roughly 500ppm Dynagro Gro/Foliage Pro or Schultz Cactus Fertilizer.

taxa.nativeRange: Pinguicula moctezumae occurs in Hidalgo and Querétaro, while P. rotundiflora is found in Nuevo León state near Aramberri.
taxa.nativeHabitat: These species inhabit vertical limestone cliffs and rock faces with persistent moisture from seepage and fog. They grow in highland pine-oak forests at elevations around 1500-2200 meters. The substrate is calcareous rock often covered with mosses and liverworts.

taxa.nativeRange: Mexican Pinguicula cultivars are derived from species native to various states in central and southern Mexico.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Tropical Mexican Pinguicula typically grow on limestone cliffs, rocky outcrops, and moss-covered surfaces in cloud forests and montane oak-pine forests. They occur at moderate to high elevations (1000-3000 m) with high humidity and seasonal moisture patterns. Substrates are usually calcareous with constant seepage or fog drip.

taxa.nativeRange: Pinguicula filifolia is endemic to western Cuba, particularly Pinar del Río province.
taxa.nativeHabitat: This species grows on wet sandstone cliffs and outcrops in pine forests at elevations from 100-700 meters. It inhabits areas with high rainfall and constant seepage over acidic to neutral substrates. Associated vegetation includes Caribbean pine (Pinus caribaea) and various ferns.

taxa.nativeRange: Pinguicula gigantea is endemic to Oaxaca and Veracruz states in southern Mexico.
taxa.nativeHabitat: This species grows on wet rocks, cliffs, and roadcuts in cloud forests at elevations from 1800-2600 meters. It inhabits areas with persistent moisture from fog and rain on substrates ranging from limestone to volcanic rock. The habitat features high humidity year-round with cool temperatures.

taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows on mossy vertical limestone cliffs and rock faces in pine-oak forests at elevations between 2,000-2,800 meters. The substrate is typically thin layers of moss over porous limestone with constant moisture from fog and mist. Occurs in shaded to partially shaded microhabitats with high humidity year-round.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to central Mexico, primarily in the states of Hidalgo, Querétaro, and San Luis Potosí in the Sierra Madre Oriental.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Found on steep limestone cliffs and rock outcrops in oak and pine-oak forests at elevations of 1,800-2,400 meters. Plants grow in thin moss cushions on vertical or overhanging rock faces with excellent drainage. The habitat receives seasonal rainfall with a distinct dry season, though fog and dew provide moisture during drier months.

taxa.nativeHabitat: True to its name, this species grows as a hemiepiphyte on mossy tree trunks and branches in humid cloud forests at elevations of 1,800-2,600 meters. The substrate consists of thick moss and organic matter accumulated on trees, maintaining constant moisture. Occurs in deeply shaded habitats within evergreen cloud forests with persistent fog and high rainfall.

Plants establish in thin moss layers over limestone with near-constant moisture from fog drip and rainfall. The heterophyllous rosettes (exhibiting distinct seasonal leaf forms) occur in partially shaded habitats with very high ambient humidity.

taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows on vertical limestone cliffs in oak-juniper woodlands at elevations of 1,200-1,800 meters. Plants establish in moss-covered crevices and on steep rock faces with excellent drainage. The habitat is characterized by hot summers with monsoon rains and cool, dry winters, representing a transition between tropical and subtropical climates.

care.temp: Temperatures from 50-85F - they prefer some humidity but can adapt to lower humidity.
care.light: Part Sun or 15W per sq ft LED light. These do well under LED lights and will attain great color.
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in ~0.5” of water using tray method. They can also be top watered regularly in humid environments, but the tray method is simpler.
care.soil: Using standard garden store materials, a mix of roughly 20% Peat Moss: 50% Perlite: 30% Vermiculite will work. I like using a mix of Perlite/Volcanic Clay Granules/Vermiculite but lots of granular, well draining mixes will work. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts. Because the roots are shallow, they do not require deep pots but pot height will influence moisture wicking to the surface.
care.feeding: Dust the leaves with Insect Powder every 2-4 weeks to stimulate explosive growth.  Foliar feeding is also good at a frequency of once or twice per month with 1/4 tsp per gallon MaxSea or roughly 500ppm Dynagro Gro/Foliage Pro or Schultz Cactus Fertilizer.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to the Tonalá region in northern Chiapas state, Mexico, near the border with Oaxaca.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows on limestone cliffs and rock outcrops in seasonally dry tropical forests at elevations of 200-800 meters. Plants establish in moss and thin soil on vertical rock faces with good drainage and seasonal moisture. The habitat experiences a pronounced dry season with monsoon rains in summer, representing a warmer, lower elevation environment than most Mexican Pinguicula.

taxa.nativeRange: Found in southern Mexico (Chiapas, Oaxaca) and Guatemala in highland regions of Central America.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows on wet mossy rocks, cliffs, and road cuts in pine-oak and cloud forests at 1500-3000m elevation. Sites have constant moisture from seepage or fog with cool to mild temperatures.

taxa.country: Portugal, Spain, France, Morocco, United Kingdom
taxa.nativeRange: Atlantic coastal regions of western Europe from Portugal and Spain north to western France, Ireland, and southwestern England; also Morocco.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in wet heathlands, bogs, and seepage areas on acidic sandy or peaty soils at low elevations. Habitats are typically coastal with mild oceanic climates and year-round moisture.

taxa.nativeRange: Circumpolar boreal distribution including Alaska, northwestern Canada, eastern Russia (Kamchatka, Siberia), and northern Japan (Hokkaido).
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in sphagnum bogs, fens, and wet alpine meadows on acidic peaty substrates. Found from sea level to subalpine zones with cold winters and cool summers in regions with high precipitation.

taxa.nativeRange: Southeastern United States coastal plain from South Carolina to Mississippi, primarily in Georgia, Florida, and Alabama.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in wet sandy soils of flatwoods, seepage slopes, and stream margins in pine savannas. Habitats are seasonally wet with acidic soils and fire-maintained vegetation at low elevations.

taxa.nativeRange: Southeastern United States coastal plain, particularly Florida panhandle, Georgia, Alabama, Mississippi, and Louisiana.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in wet sandy peat soils of seepage bogs, wet pine savannas, and pond margins. Habitats have acidic substrates with seasonal flooding and fire-maintained open canopy at low elevations.

taxa.nativeRange: Southeastern United States coastal plain from North Carolina to Texas, concentrated in Georgia, Florida, Alabama, and Louisiana.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows in wet sandy soils of seepage areas, pond margins, and wet flatwoods in pine savannas. Habitats are seasonally saturated with acidic substrates and periodic fire disturbance.

taxa.country: USA, Mexico, Central America, South America, Africa, Asia, Australia
taxa.nativeRange: Cosmopolitan species found across tropical, subtropical, and warm temperate regions on multiple continents including North and South America, Africa, Asia, and Australia.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Grows as free-floating or loosely rooted aquatic in shallow ponds, ditches, rice paddies, and slow streams. Prefers nutrient-poor acidic to neutral water in open sunny habitats from sea level to moderate elevations.

taxa.nativeRange: Eastern United States coastal plain from New Jersey to Texas, most common in southeastern states from Virginia to Louisiana.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Free-floating aquatic in shallow acidic ponds, lakes, and slow-moving streams in pine flatwoods and wetlands. Forms distinctive inflated floating whorls and grows in nutrient-poor waters at low elevations.

taxa.nativeRange: Eastern North America from Nova Scotia and Quebec south through the Atlantic coastal plain to Florida and west to the Great Lakes region and Texas.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Free-floating aquatic in acidic ponds, lakes, and slow streams, often in association with sphagnum. Grows in nutrient-poor waters of bogs and coastal plain wetlands from sea level to moderate elevations.

taxa.nativeRange: Andean cloud forests from Venezuela through Colombia and Ecuador to Peru, primarily at high elevations in the northern Andes.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Epiphytic on mossy tree trunks and branches in montane cloud forests at 1800-3000m elevation. Grows in constantly humid environments with frequent fog and cool temperatures on moss-covered substrates.

care.temp: Temperatures of 60-95F at all humidity levels.
care.light: Part Sun or at least 10W-15W per sq ft LED light. This species can tolerate brighter light and seems to be fine.
care.water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Keep the soil constantly wet and do not allow it to dry out. This species does best for me with good drainage and shallow trays.
care.soil: 50% Peat Moss: 50% Sand/Perlite. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts.
care.feeding: Feed with MaxSea or Dynagro fertilizer diluted to 1/4 tsp per gallon (or roughly 200-400ppm) every month. Lightly spray the leaves but do not overfeed, especially in undrained containers.

taxa.nativeRange: Endemic to a small area within coastal North and South Carolina, primarily within a 75-mile radius of Wilmington, North Carolina.
taxa.nativeHabitat: Found in nutrient-poor, acidic, moist soils of longleaf pine savannas and pocosins. Grows in wet sandy or peaty substrates with high water tables, often in areas subject to periodic fire. Associated with other carnivorous plants including sundews and pitcher plants in open, sunny habitats.
taxa.provenanceWarning: Dionaea 'Big Dracula' appears to be a cultivar or trade name for a Venus flytrap variety, not a distinct species. Dionaea is a monotypic genus containing only Dionaea muscipula. Reliable specific information about this particular cultivar's characteristics, origin, or breeder is not available in scientific literature. Native range and habitat data provided are for the species Dionaea muscipula, as all cultivars share this origin.

Pygmy.light: Light: Full Sun or 20W per sq ft LED light. Pygmy sundews love bright light.  Seasonal cues are essential for Pygmy Sundews to produce gemmae in fall.
Pygmy.water: Water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in 0.5”-1” of water using tray method.
Pygmy.soil: Soil: 40% Peat: 60% Perlite/Sand is a good mix. Pygmy Sundews like well draining soils. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts.
Pygmy.dormancy: Dormancy not required, however extreme heat or dryness can induce summer brumation.
Pygmy.temp: Climate: Mediterranean (SW Australia). 40-100F, low to moderate humidity.

Annual.temp: Climate: Temperatures from 60-100F.  These prefer moderate humidity but can easily acclimate to low humidity.
Annual.light: Light: Part to Full Sun or 20W per sq ft LED light.  Plant in the sun will be more colorful, shorter, and more robust.
Annual.water: Water: Distilled or Reverse Osmosis water. Sitting in 0.5”-2” of water using tray method.
Annual.soil: Soil: 40% Peat Moss: 40% Sand: 20% Perlite or other well draining peat + sand mixes. Rinse media with distilled or reverse osmosis water to remove excess salts.
Annual.feeding: Feeding: Plants outdoors will catch lots of flies on their own. Feed Insect Powder or roughly 400ppm foliar fertilization if they are not catching enough or if growing indoors.  Dilute soil fertilization also works well.
Annual.dormancy: No dormancy but these are annuals that will mass flower and slowly die.
